DISCOVERY 302, DEFENDANTS JENI FONG AND HOWARD WONG'S MOTION TO QUASH BUSINESS RECORDS SUBPOENAS TO TERENCE CHOW, CPA, AND HATTER & CO., CPA. DEFENDANT'S MOTION IS GRANTED IN PART AND DENIED IN PART. PLAINTIFF'S SUBPOENA CALLS FOR THE PRODUCTIONS OF DEFENDANTS' TAX RETURN INFORMATION, WHICH IS PRIVILEGED MATERIAL AND PLAINTIFF HAS NOT DEMONSTRATED ANY BASIS FOR INVADING THIS PRIVILEGE. ACCORDINGLY, THE SUBPOENA MUST BE MODIFIED TO STRIKE THE PRODUCTION OF THIS PRIVILEGED MATERIAL. REQUESTS 1 THROUGH 4 SHOULD STRIKE THE PHRASE "AND/OR TAX RETURNS." REQUEST 5 MUST BE STRICKEN IN ITS ENTIRETY. DISCOVERY OF THE COMPANY'S PREVIOUS FINANCIAL HISTORY COULD REASONABLY LEAD TO THE DISCOVERY OF RELEVANT EVIDENCE, BUT THIS MUST BE BALANCED ON THE NEED TO MINIMIZE THE BURDEN ON THIRD PARTIES, ESPECIALLY THIS EARLY IN THE LITIGATION. COMMUNICATIONS BETWEEN THE TAX PREPARER AND THE CLIENT CAN BE PRIVILEGED, PRESENT A HEAVY BURDEN TO THE THIRD PARTIES, AND ARE UNLIKELY TO YIELD RELEVANT EVIDENCE. REQUESTS FOR COMMUNICATIONS MUST ALSO BE STRICKEN (REQUESTS 6-8 AND 12, AND PARTS OF 9-11.) REQUESTS FOR DOCUMENTS IN NUMBERS 9-11 ARE LESS BURDENSOME AND ARE REASONABLY CALCULATED TO LEAD TO RELEVANT EVIDENCE. PLAINTIFF SHALL REISSUE AND SERVE THE SUBPOENAS CONSISTENT WITH THIS ORDER. ORDER IS SIGNED. JUDGE PRO TEM: CONOR MOORE, CLERK: S. LE, NOT REPORTED. =(302/JPT)

Mario A Moya, Esq of Moya law, 510-926-6521, appearing for Defendants, Jeni Fong et al; for Defendant.
No other Appearance.

RULING - The matter is Uncontested. The Court adopts the tentative ruling as follows:

PLAINTIFF GRACE IMAGE PHOTOGRAPHY, JUDY MCKIE'S Motion For Preliminary Injunction Prohibiting Defendants Fong and Wong from destroying evidence.

Plaintiffs move under CCP Sec. 526(a)(3) to enjoin defendants from "destroying, deleting or secreting relevant evidence." The motion is denied. CCP Sec. 526(a)(3) may be used to enjoin acts in litigation that violate "the rights of another party," and, though not a tight fit, the statute has been applied to discovery. (Id.; Dodge, Warren & Peters Ins. Services, Inc. v. Riley (105 Cal.App. 4th 114, 1418-19.) However, Sec. 526(a)(3) speaks in the present tense - "is doing," "is about to do" - while the last "destroying, deleting or secreting" plaintiffs allege was in April 2018, over 10 months ago. The delay in Dodge was not nearly so protracted. A panoply of discovery and evidentiary sanctions are available if plaintiffs show discovery abuse, but CCP Sec. 526(a)(3) does not apply here.

Order is signed. (see scanned order for details)

DEMURRER TO COMPLAINT
Off Calendar Dec-14-2018 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES:
Appearances:
Peter Bertrand, Esq., Buchalter, Telephone 415-227-0900; apearing for Plaintiff;
Mario A. Moya, Esq, Moya Law, Telephone 510-926-6521, appearing for Defendants Jeni Fong & Howard Wong;
Rebecca M. Hoberg, Esq, Moya Law, Telephone 510-926-6521, appearing for Defendants Jeni Fong & Howard Wong;

Ruling - ARGUED. The Court adopts the tentative ruling as follows:

Defendants Jeni Fong, Howard Wong and May-Lee Chai's demurrers are all OVERRULED.
The complaint is thorough and comprehensive. Defendants repeatedly ignore fundamental rules governing demurrers; among them: (1) the truth of all pleaded facts is assumed, (2) if even one of several grounds for relief is properly pled, the demurrer is denied as to a cause of action and (3) defendants may not adduce or argue extrinsic evidence in a demurrer. As to the complaint's numbered causes of action:
1-3: Breaches of contract and the implied covenant, and inducing breach, are all more than adequately pled.
4: Fraudulent inducement is pled with adequate specificity.
5-6, 12, 14-15: Trade secrets are pled with adequate particularity. Defendants dispute the pleaded facts, which is not permitted in a demurrer. A trade secret claim only preempts claims based on identical facts.
7: Unjust enrichment and/or quasi-contract are adequately pled. A defendant need not be a signatory to a contract in order to be unjustly enriched.
8: Ownership of the computer services is adequately alleged.
9: The complaint pleads the elements of intentional interference with actual and prospective economic relations.
10: The complaint pleads that defendant Chai knowingly gave substantial assistance to the efforts to interfere with plaintiffs' business; that suffices.
11: A duty to defendant is not required for conspiracy. (Stueve Bros. Farms, LLC v. Berger Kahn (2013) 222 Cal.App.4th 303, 323.)
13: Plaintiff pleads conduct that, if proven, can be viewed as outrageous and extreme.

Prevailing party to submit proposed order.

Judge: Richard B. Ulmer; Clerk: W. Trupek; Court Reporter: Maria Torreano CSR#8600, email: maria.torreano@gmail.com (302/HEK)

Notice Of Motion And Motion By Defendants Jeni Fong And Howard Wong To Quash Business Records Subpoenas To Terence Chow, Cpa, And Hatter & Co., Cpa
Off Calendar Dec-07-2018 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES:
Appearances:
Rebecca Hoberg, esq. (415-378-0086) of Moya Law Firm for Defendants JENI FONG and HOWARD WONG.
No other appearances.

The Court adopts the tentative ruling as follows:

DEFENDANTs JENI FONG and HOWARD WONG's Motion To Quash Business Records Subpoenas To Terence Chow, CPA, And Hatter & Co., CPA.

Defendant's motion is granted in part and denied in part. Plaintiff's subpoena calls for the productions of Defendants' tax return information, which is privileged material and Plaintiff has not demonstrated any basis for invading this privilege. Accordingly, the subpoena must be modified to strike the production of this privileged material. Requests 1 through 4 should strike the phrase "and/or tax returns." Request 5 must be stricken in its entirety. Discovery of the company's previous financial history could reasonably lead to the discovery of relevant evidence, but this must be balanced on the need to minimize the burden on third parties, especially this early in the litigation. Communications between the tax preparer and the client can be privileged, present a heavy burden to the third parties, and are unlikely to yield relevant evidence. Requests for communications must also be stricken (Requests 6-8 and 12, and parts of 9-11.) Requests for documents in numbers 9-11 are less burdensome and are reasonably calculated to lead to relevant evidence. Plaintiff shall reissue and serve the subpoenas consistent with this order.

Order is signed.

Judge Pro Tem: Conor Moore, Clerk: S. Le, Not reported. =(302/JPT)
